coredata.dc:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots derived by cationic surfactant for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Navneet
Mehta, Akansha
Mishra, Amit
Chaudhary, Savita
Rawat, Mohit
Basu, Soumen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Dots (CDs) the kind of recently exposed fluorescent nanomaterials have become increasingly popular in the precedent decade due to their distinctive physical and optical properties. Relating to above recognition for the first time we present the synthesis of CDs by cationic surfactant, Cetylpyridinium Bromide (CPB). Due to good carbon content amphiphilicity, and existence of heteroaromatic π system, CPB reveals three advantageous properties including a good carbon source, stabilizing agent, and contributing fluorophore in the CDs system. The as prepared CDs synthesized by hydrothermal technique reveals excellent fluorescent properties having strong green emission at 525 nm when excited over 470 nm. The FTIR results showed the presence of CC, CO, NH, CH and OH bonds. The presence of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface confer them water solubility. The HRTEM results revealed the size of prepared CDs to be in the range of 7–10 nm. The XPS spectrum confirms the presence of Carbon, Oxygen and Nitrogen, suggesting that the CDs have good purity and very little impurities. Latterly CDs were used for the selective and sensitive detection towards Fe2+ ions. Also the as prepared CDs were utilized for real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cationic surfactant
Detection of metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of CQDs, (b) PL emission spectra of CQDs excited at 470 nm, (c) effect of excitation wavelength on the emission behaviour of CQDs and (d) FTIR spectra of CQDs.
XRD pattern, (b) HRTEM, (c) particle size distribution and (d) SAED pattern of as-prepared CQDs.
(a) XPS scan of CQDs, (b) PL spectra of CQDs dispersed in solutions of different pH, (c) selectivity investigation of CQDs with different metal ions and (d) Stern Volmer plot of Fe2+ ions.
The decrease in fluorescence intensity of CQDs with the increase concentration of (a) Fericip iron tablet and (b) Grefer iron syrup.
Comparison of present study with published reports for detection of Fe2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots derived by cationic surfactant for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar University, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Contribution of first and second author is equal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots (CDs) the kind of recently exposed fluorescent nanomaterials have become increasingly popular in the precedent decade due to their distinctive physical and optical properties. Relating to above recognition for the first time we present the synthesis of CDs by cationic surfactant, Cetylpyridinium Bromide (CPB). Due to good carbon content amphiphilicity, and existence of heteroaromatic π system, CPB reveals three advantageous properties including a good carbon source, stabilizing agent, and contributing fluorophore in the CDs system. The as prepared CDs synthesized by hydrothermal technique reveals excellent fluorescent properties having strong green emission at 525 nm when excited over 470 nm. The FTIR results showed the presence of CC, CO, NH, CH and OH bonds. The presence of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface confer them water solubility. The HRTEM results revealed the size of prepared CDs to be in the range of 7–10 nm. The XPS spectrum confirms the presence of Carbon, Oxygen and Nitrogen, suggesting that the CDs have good purity and very little impurities. Latterly CDs were used for the selective and sensitive detection towards Fe2+ ions. Also the as prepared CDs were utilized for real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are the fluorescent carbon materials with average size between 1 and 10 nm have attracted great attention and have been considered as much safer and biocompatible alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots such as ZnS/CdSe QDs which due to their toxic nature are causing threat both to environment and living beings. Moreover, other limitations of photo luminescent counterparts such as degradation of dye, photo bleaching and use of heavy metals are also overcome by CDs which due to their non toxicity, unique fluorescent properties, high aqueous solubility and biocompatibility, plenty of raw materials in nature, photo stability and easy efficient synthesis are promising substitutes for fluorescent QDs. These advantages accompanied by presence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs make them a suitable candidate for the applications starting from sensing to bioimaging [1], phototransistors and photo catalysis [2], heavy metal ion sensing [3,4], explosion detection [5,6], light harvesting [7], biomedicine [8] etc.

Basically, the nature of photoluminescence exhibited by carbon quantum dots are influenced by their size, morphologies and composition which are determined by both the precursor and the synthesis route adopted for the fabrication of CDs. The synthesis method of CDs is categorized into two routes: (a) physical synthesis methods, like laser ablation [9–11], arc discharge [12], and plasma induced [13] and (b) the chemical synthesis methods, like electrochemical synthesis [14–17], hydrothermal [18–20], solvothermal [21,22], pyrolysis [23–25], microwave-assisted [26–28], and support assisted [29,30]. Different synthesis methods result in formation of CDs which comprises of amorphous to crystalline carbon structures that are sp2 hybridized to diamond like structures which contain sp3 hybridized carbon. However, these synthesis routes possess major disadvantages of being expensive, involving complex and time consuming reactions, hard synthesis conditions and making use of toxic chemicals. In this paper, CDs have been synthesized by using hydrothermal method. There are various advantages of using this route including easy process ability at ambient conditions, cost efficient, rapid, uses of inexpensive and easy to handle apparatus, low energy consumption and produce carbon dots with very good photo luminescent properties. Typically in this synthesis route the organic precursor is sealed and reacted in hydrothermal reactor at high temperature. Cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) is a cationic surfactant having a quaternary nitrogen atom at its heterocyclic ring at its head and consists of 16 carbon atoms at its tail. It is well known for its germicidal properties and it has diverse applications in protein folding, polymerization, enzyme studies, gene and drug delievery [1]. Surfactants such as CBP play very crucial role in both stabilizing nanostructures and in formation of organic and inorganic nanoporous structures [31,32]. Due to high carbon content and presence of multifunctional groups CBP can serve as promising precursors for the fabrication of CDs. These days the interest of researchers is increasing on carbon materials for biosensing applications. Because of its unusual properties, facile synthesis from abundant and inexpensive starting materials are preferred. The few reports highlight a panorama of the latest advancements in sensing applications for different metal ions [33–36].

In view of above work, here we report the preparation and characterization of photo luminescent CDs synthesized by hydrothermal route by using cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) as precursor. The as-prepared CDs showed excitation wavelength behaviour and were being used for selectivity and sensitivity of Fe2+ metal ions (Table 1 ).

Cetylpyridinium Bromide (C21H38BrN) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as a starting material. Other reagents that were used included Hydrogen Peroxide, H2O2 (Fisher Scientific), Ferrous Chloride, FeCl2 (Emsure, Merck), pH buffers of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 (Merck) and Deionized water, H2O (Sigma Aldrich).

The synthesis procedure involved weighing 1 g of Cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) and adding it to 50 mL of water. Then the mixture was transferred to autoclave and 2.5 mL of hydrogen peroxide was added to the solution. Finally the resulting mixture was kept in oven for 4 h at 180 °C. The black- brown coloured precipitate was obtained which was then dried to obtain dry light brown powder. The obtained CDs showed green luminescence when placed in UV lamp.

The use of surfactants as a carbon source was motivated by their applicability as nanoparticle stabilizing shells that might be hydrothermally turned into carbon shells, enhancing the photoluminescence (PL) and/or amphiphilicity of the formed materials. Surfactants as multifunctional compounds with a high carbon content and heteroatom-containing functionalities are promising precursors for the synthesis of CDs. The process of hydrothermal carbonization generally involves series of reactions including hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, polymerization, aromatization [37]. The basic reaction conditions and high temperature causes the dehydrogenation of alkyl chains, which is further followed by the formation of aromatic structures within the carbonaceous core. The addition of H2O2 during synthesis may have led to introduction of oxygen functionalities, since H2O2 is highly unstable and breaks down in the presence of light to release O2 and also forms reactive oxygen species such as OH−. This may result in the formation of amphiphilic CQDs. The resulting sp2 domains embedded in the sp3 matrix are considered to be the “intrinsic” luminescence centres [38,39].

The procedure used for detection of Fe2+ is as following: the photoluminescence spectrum of 2 mL of CPB-CDs (control) was recorded and then 1 mL of different chloride metal ions of 25 ppm including Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Li2+, K2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Sn2+and Fe2+ were added to control. However, only Fe2+ ions showed the most quenching of the fluorescence and no tremendous quenching was observed for other metal ions. All detection experiments were carried out under same conditions at room temperature. To evaluate CDs based sensor for Fe2+ detection in 2 mL of control, different concentrations of Fe2+ (100 μL, 200 μL, 300 μL, 400 μL, 500 μL, 600 μL, 700 μL, 800 μL, 900 μL, 1000 μL) were mixed under gentle shaking. It was observed that with increase in concentration of Fe2+, fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases (Fig. 4 and S2). However, beyond the concentration limit of 1000 μL, the solution achieved saturation and does not showed any variation in florescence intensity with further increase in concentration.

UV–visible spectra were recorded by using Shimadzu/UV-2600 spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra of CDs were recorded by Bruker Alpha-T spectrophotometer. The PL spectra of CQDs were recorded by using FLS980-S2S2-s (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., United Kingdom) spectrophotometer. High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations were carried out on JEOL JE-2100 microscope at 200 kV operating voltage. The phase identification was analyzed through XRD analysis (PANALYTICAL X'Pert PRO) with Cu K α (λ = 1.540 Å) as radiation source over the range of 10° ≤ 2θ ≤ 90°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on the Perkin Elmer PE-983 FTIR spectrophotometer. The oxidation state was analyzed by X ray photon spectroscopy (XPS, KRATOS Axis 165 Shimadzu, UK) with Mg Ka radiation (1252.6 eV at 75 W).

The UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded within the range from 250 to 500 nm by a spectrophotometer using quartz cuvette with 1 cm optical path. The Fig. 1(a) shows that the CDs solution exhibit absorption band at 259 nm which is attributed to n-π* transition of CO and π to π* transition of the conjugated CC bands which are characteristic to CDs. The resultant CDs displayed a typical excitation-dependent PL, recorded by using PL spectrophotometer. The Fig. 1(b) and (c) show that CDs achieved the maximum emission intensity around 525 nm (a bright green emission) when it was excited at 470 nm. When the excitation wavelength increased from 470 nm to 560 nm with the increment of 10 nm, the emission peak shifted towards the higher wavelength but a decrease in intensity was also observed as shown in Fig. 1(c). There are two main reasons for this behavior. Quantum confinement effect in CQDs results in the discretization of bands and also increases the gap between HOMO and LUMO, hence smaller size CQDs get excited at lower wavelengths and vice versa. Firstly, the presence of different particle sizes. The particles with a smaller size get excited at a lower wavelength, whereas those with a larger size get excited at higher wavelengths. The intensity of the PL depends on the number of particles excited at a particular wavelength, this means that at 470 nm maximum number of particles was excited. Secondly, the nature of their surface (surface defects), which also influence the emission of CDs. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of the CDs may result in a series of emissive traps between π and π* of CC. On illuminating the CDs at a certain excitation wavelength a surface energy trap dominates the emission. As the excitation wavelength changes, other corresponding surface state emissive traps become dominant. For CDs, the values of colour chromaticity coordinates are found to be x = 0.32646 and y = 0.46079. These coordinates represent the green colour region in the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram shown in Fig. S1 (in supporting information). The green emission arises from both an intrinsic band gap resulting from confined sp2 conjugation in the core of CDs and extrinsic fluorescence resulting from surface state that can be either directly excited or excited by energy transfer from the intrinsic band.

The FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 1(d) were collected in the transmission range of 700 cm−1 to 3800 cm−1. The absorption peak emerging at 3673 cm−1 and 3614 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of OH. The peaks at 2928 cm−1, 2859 cm−1 and 776 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibration. The absorption bands at 1917 cm−1 represent the = C-H vibrations while those appearing at 1744 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibrations. The strong broad vibration at around 1696 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 are attributed to CC stretch of polycyclic hydrocarbons and NH bending vibrations respectively. The presence of the hydroxyl (OH) and carboxyl (CO) (hydrophilic) groups on the surface of CDs imparts excellent water solubility and stability. Also, the presence of both hydrophilic groups (OH and CO) and hydrophobic groups (CC, CH and CH) impart amphiphilic nature to CQDs.

The thin film XRD spectrum of CDs shows a broad peak centered at 2θ = 26.27° which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms. The crystallite size of CDs calculated from Scherrer equation (D = 0.9λ/βcosθ) was found to be 7 nm which was in accordance with HR-TEM results. The morphological studies of the prepared CDs were done with HR-TEM as shown in Fig. 2(b). The HR-TEM images as shown in Fig. 2(c) and S4 shows irregular spherical shaped CDs within the size range of 7–10 nm. The survey scan of the XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3(a), exhibit three distinct peaks 284.52 eV, 401.7 eV and 532.26 eV which corresponds to carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1 s) and oxygen (O1s), respectively. The elemental components of synthesized CDs are mainly C (92.54%), O (4.29%) and N (3.15%). From high-resolution spectra of C1s the peak present at 284.98 eV is assigned to adventitious carbon and at 286.61 to the presence of CO bond (Fig. S3(a)). The peak at 401.75 eV in high-resolution spectrum of N1 s (Fig. S3 (b)) is assigned to the presence of C-N-H bond. The peak at 531.80 eV in XPS of O1s (Fig. S3(c)) is ascribed to the presence of OH groups.

The pH behavior of the CDs was also explored in this work. The synthesized CDs possess very sensitive nature to change in pH of the solution. The pH of the sample was set to 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 and its effects are demonstrated in Fig. 3(b). It was observed that as the pH was increased, the fluorescence intensity was decreased linearly when excited to 470 nm. This behavior can be attributed to change in medium from acidic to alkaline resulting in protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl group causing the electrostatic charging of CDs. This charging subsequently causes a shift in Fermi level. The electronic transitions of π–π* and σ-σ* are changed by replenishing or draining their valance bands with the variation in pH. Even though its mechanism is not completely understood, this optically pH-sensitive property could be exploited for the fluorescence based pH sensors.

Fluorescence quenching is an effective mechanism to detect and quantify various metal ions for purpose of benefiting both environment and health of living beings. For this purpose sensitivity and selectivity test of different metal ions including Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Li2+, K2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Sn2+and Fe2+ was conducted. All detection experiments were carried out under the same conditions at room temperature. The experiment procedure involved taking 2 mL of control (i.e. CDs solution) and adding 1 mL of 25 ppm different metal ion solutions every time. However, only Fe2+ ions showed the most quenching of the fluorescence and no tremendous quenching was observed for other metal ions. The Fig. 3(c, d) shows the variation in the photoluminescence intensity of synthesized CDs with the variation of Fe2+ ions concentration and their respective Stern-Volmer plot for Fe2+ sensing.

This study is further used to calculate the detection limit of Fe2+ ions using CPB derived CDs. The detection limits are calculated using the following equation.
Detection Limit = 3 × Standard Deviation / Slope

Using above equation, the calculated detection limit for Fe2+ ions is 0.150 ppm with standard deviation R2 = 0.94405. So the synthesized CDs are highly significant for detection of this metal ions as from the experimental study, it is observed that very small concentrations of Fe2+ ions can be detected.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be ~34.61% when investigated by comparing the integrated intensities (excited at 470 nm) and the absorbance value of CDs at 470 nm with reference quinine sulphate (QS) in 0.01 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%):
QY S
=
QY R
×
η R 2
η S 2
×
I S
A S
×
A R
I R
where, QY, I, A, and η are assigned to QY, integrated PL intensity, absorbance values, and refractive index, respectively. The subscript R and S refer to reference and sample.

The sensing ability of synthesized CDs was investigated on real samples of iron tablet and syrup. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed to decrease with increase in the concentration of iron tablet and syrup increases. Thus, showing the efficiency of synthesized CDs to be used in the application of iron sensors.

CDs were synthesized successfully using hydrothermal approach using surfactant Cetylpyridinium Bromide (CPB) as carbon source. The CDs were characterized for their optical, structural and morphological properties. The UV–Visible spectra exhibited absorption band at 259 nm for the synthesized CDs and the quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be ~34.61%. The spherical CDs having average diameter of 7–10 nm were found to be partially monodispersed and stable for 8–10 months. The synthesized CDs were found to be sensitive towards different pH buffer solutions over range of 1 to 11. The CDs possessed great selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe2+ ions present in the solution due the quenching in the fluorescence intensity of CDs fluorescence. The calculated detection limit for Fe2+ ions was 0.7541 ppm with standard deviation of R2 = 0.94405. Thus it can be concluded that the CDs can be explored for the fabrication of nanoprobe for metal ion detection.
The authors declare no intellectual as well as financial conflict of interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.10.058.
